input.query_title: Ising Model on Networks with an Arbitrary Distribution of Connections
input.query_abstract: We find the exact critical temperature $T_c$ of the nearest-neighbor ferromagnetic Ising model on an `equilibrium' random graph with an arbitrary degree distribution $P(k)$. We observe an anomalous behavior of the magnetization, magnetic susceptibility and specific heat, when $P(k)$ is fat-tailed, or, loosely speaking, when the fourth moment of the distribution diverges in infinite networks. When the second moment becomes divergent, $T_c$ approaches infinity, the phase transition is of infinite order, and size effect is anomalously strong.
input.hyp_generic_object: binary solvent/default states of institutions coupled through an interbank exposure network with an arbitrary distribution of counterparties
input.hyp_abstract: We compute in closed form the critical level of financial stress at which an interbank system tips from stability into a system-wide default cascade, for a counterparty network whose number of exposures per institution follows an arbitrary distribution P(k). Each institution is treated as a binary unit, either solvent or in default, its condition responding to the states of its counterparties through shared exposures, and the contagion is resolved via a self-consistent equation for the fraction of the system in default. The resilience threshold separating a shock absorbed locally from one that propagates to engulf the whole network is governed by the moments of the exposure distribution, scaling with the ratio of the mean number of counterparties to its second moment. When the exposure network is heavy-tailed — a market organized around a few enormously interconnected hub institutions — the equilibrium default fraction, its sensitivity to a marginal deterioration in conditions, and the spread of cascade sizes all behave anomalously as the second moment of the connectivity distribution diverges. In that limit the resilience threshold falls toward zero, leaving the system fragile to vanishingly small shocks; the passage from stability to collapse loses its sharp character and becomes anomalously smooth, and finite-system corrections remain large even for markets with many participants. We draw consequences for macroprudential capital surcharges levied on systemically central institutions.
input.candidate_abstract: In the last few years we have witnessed the emergence, primarily in on-line communities, of new types of social networks that require for their representation more complex graph structures than have been employed in the past. One example is the folksonomy, a tripartite structure of users, resources, and tags -- labels collaboratively applied by the users to the resources in order to impart meaningful structure on an otherwise undifferentiated database. Here we propose a mathematical model of such tripartite structures which represents them as random hypergraphs. We show that it is possible to calculate many properties of this model exactly in the limit of large network size and we compare the results against observations of a real folksonomy, that of the on-line photography web site Flickr. We show that in some cases the model matches the properties of the observed network well, while in others there are significant differences, which we find to be attributable to the practice of multiple tagging, i.e., the application by a single user of many tags to one resource, or one tag to many resources.
